findings: W's balancing takeout double band floored at 8 HCP; the card's balancing floor is 12-3=9, so the core belongs at 9-11 with 7-8 as a light-margin tail.

deviations.2S: suggests a sixth spade after the 1S opening and 2C rebid; here only five to the KQJ

S.note: 1NT response to 1S (6-10, at most two spades), then passed 2C to show club preference, usually four but sometimes three

W.note: passed hand; balancing-style takeout double of 2C showing both red suits and club shortness, roughly 9-11 (the card's balancing floor), occasionally lighter

E.note: passed over 1S and took no action later: no 1NT overcall, no suitable suit bid or takeout double; up to a bad 14

patched_doc.explanation: West's balancing double announces the red suits and club shortness, so the deal belongs to whoever judges the trump situation best. Passing keeps 2C doubled in play when East holds club length behind dummy, but it also invites East to escape to a red suit at a comfortable level. 3C uses the fourth trump to buy the contract cheaply and puts maximum pressure on East, whose values are limited by the earlier passes. 2S is tempting with KQJ76, yet it advertises a sixth spade opposite a partner who has already denied a fit, so it stakes the board on suit quality rather than fit. The question is whether fighting for the partscore with the known nine-card club fit beats defending or retreating to the strong five-card suit.

findings: Hero holds 7 HCP (ace of hearts, king of clubs), not 6 as the deviation note and explanation claim.
1H tree parked partner's 18-19 jump raise in 3HN, but with 7 HCP and a fifth trump hero accepts the invite; game/partial thresholds rebuilt (17+ reaches 4H, 14-16 stops in 3H).

W passed over the takeout double as responder, which caps far below 9: a pass over X is roughly 0-5 with an occasional trapped 6-8, and it also denies a diamond raise; hcp and diamond bands tightened.

deviations.2H: only 7 HCP; the jump advance of a takeout double shows 9-11

S.note: takeout double of 1D: opening values with diamond shortness and support for the unbid suits, or a hand of 17+ with any shape

W.note: passed as dealer, then passed again as responder over the double: no redouble, no bid, no diamond raise; nearly always under six points

patched_doc.explanation: Raw count says 7 HCP and a minimum 1H advance, but takeout-double evaluation is about tricks: a fifth trump, an ace, a working king behind the opening bidder, and a doubleton all argue the hand plays much better than it counts. The jump to 2H tells partner exactly that, and lets a sound doubler with a shapely 14-15 bid a vulnerable game that the quiet 1H may bury, since the doubler needs real extras to move over 1H. The cost is equally clear: when partner is a flat minimum double, 2H has overstated the hand by a trick and the three level may already be too high. Note that 2H is a card violation here, so partner will drive on hands that expect 9-11 opposite. The trade-off is missed vulnerable games against going plus quietly in a partscore.

findings: 1D/1S trees decide heart declarership only off the doubler; when advancer holds four hearts he would often name them first, but the doubler declares only on the big-hand layouts, so the approximation is acceptable.
Penalty conversion of 1Cx on five clubs to modest honors is on the loose side, but the branch carries weight only on genuine trap layouts.
Missing option a panel would demand: the natural 2C rebid on KQT54 is a mainstream escape from the stolen 1NT rebid; added with its own tree and a suit-length deviation note.
Unflagged deviation: the takeout-shaped double delivers only three hearts (QJ8); a low-level takeout double of 1S normally promises four cards in the unbid major.
Explanation extended to acknowledge both newly flagged deviations.

deviations.X: opener's takeout-shaped double here normally delivers four hearts; QJ8 is only three
deviations.1NT: the 1NT rebid over interference wants a real spade stopper; bare QJ is a coin-flip
deviations.2C: a free 2C rebid over the overcall typically shows six clubs; this is a good five

W.note: 1D response to 1C: 6+ points with four-plus diamonds, occasionally shaded, strength unlimited but usually a modest hand

N.note: 1S overcall: 8-16 with a decent five-plus spade suit

patched_doc.explanation: Opener has a comfortable minimum with clubs and scattered values, and North's 1S overcall has stolen the convenient rebid. The takeout-flavored double shows this skeleton, doubleton spade with heart support and diamond tolerance, and keeps both red suits in play at the two level, though with only three hearts it is a card light of the classic shape and partner may leap in hearts expecting four. 1NT describes the balanced 12-count and the general strength but leans on QJ tight as a stopper, which wins when the lead runs to the closed hand and loses ugly when spades come pouring in. 2C retreats to the chunky five-card suit, the least committal noise, but it normally promises a sixth club and can bury a red-suit fit. Pass is the disciplined minimum action, planning to compete later, but the auction may return at an uncomfortable height with the fit still unfound. The choice is really about how hard a minimum balanced opener should work once the opponents own the boss suit.

findings: X tree folds partner's forced major-suit responses back into 2NT, which matches the explanation's 'corrected to 2NT, two lies told' story; the trees still separate via the penalty-conversion branch and the different game thresholds.
QJ543 fifth counts as a working stopper for the 2NT invite given the length sitting behind the overcall; no deviation flag needed.
P tree ignored opener's routine reopening double when short in clubs — the exact trap-pass payoff the explanation sells (QT953 sitting over the bidder); conversion branch added.
X tree's else had opener introducing a possibly three-card diamond suit; split into 2DE with four-plus diamonds, otherwise a natural 2NTE.

deviations.X: the negative double implies four cards in both unbid majors; the singleton spade risks an unwelcome 2S reply

N.note: passed as dealer: no opening bid, no preempt

S.note: natural 2C overcall of 1NT: a decent five-plus club suit, roughly 8-15

patched_doc.explanation: Six points would normally pass without a thought, but this hand has two features that pull in opposite directions: four respectable hearts that want to compete, and QT953 of clubs sitting directly over the overcaller, which whispers that 2C may be going nowhere. The negative double hunts the 4-4 heart fit and lets partner convert for penalties with club length, a lovely parlay when it works. Its flaw is the singleton spade: partner will bid 2S with four of them and no hearts, and there is no safe harbor afterwards. Passing scores quietly whenever 2C struggles on its own, and it is not purely passive either: a short-club opener will often reopen with a double that this trump holding is delighted to convert. This is a classic trap-pass versus fit-hunt decision at low level.

findings: P tree omitted opener's reopening double when short in diamonds; with 9865 in their suit hero pulls a reopening double to 2H, so the pass line still finds the heart fit on those layouts (branch added, contract 2HS).

deviations.X: the negative double implies four cards in both unbid majors; only two spades here

W.note: passed as dealer: no opening bid

E.note: natural 2D overcall of 1NT: a decent five-plus diamond suit, roughly 8-15

patched_doc.explanation: With a working six-count and four hearts, the negative double is the textbook way to stay in the auction: it finds the 4-4 heart fit when partner has one and lets a strong notrump with good diamonds convert to penalty at favorable colors for the defense. The catch is the shape, since only two spades means partner's routine 2S response lands the partnership in a seven-card fit with no graceful exit, and 9865 of diamonds contributes nothing to a penalty pass. Passing concedes 2D quietly, which is fine when the deal is a misfit, and it is not always the end of the story: a short-diamond opener will often reopen with a double, letting this hand show the hearts after all. But when opener is obliged to sit, a heart partscore that belongs to your side may vanish. Partner still holds 15-17, so selling out at the two level cheaply is not automatic. The simulation will say whether the flawed double or the meek pass loses less over the long run.

findings: Responder's 2NT in a 2/1 game-forcing auction is unlimited; the meaning capped at 19 and excludes the actual 20-count that produced the room's 6NT — margin extended to 20.

deviations.3H: shows three-card heart support; here it is a doubleton AK

N.note: 2H game-force with five-plus hearts, then a natural 2NT: balanced-ish, at most two spades, minimum 12 up to a big balanced hand of 20

W.note: passed throughout with no overcall or action

patched_doc.explanation: In a two-over-one auction the game force is already established, so the real question is which strain and how much room to leave. 3NT is the honest, fast-arrival call with a 5-2-2-3 twelve-count and soft minor-suit holdings, but it slams the door: partner with the big balanced rebid can only guess with a quantitative raise. The false preference to 3H, on AK doubleton, keeps the auction alive below game; those two trumps are worth more than many three-card holdings, and hearing partner's next move may be the only route to a laydown slam when the 2NT rebid conceals a big balanced hand. The risk is playing a 5-2 fit at the four level, or higher, when partner is minimum and notrump was the spot. Note the deviation: partner will genuinely believe in the eight-card fit, and the simulation will price both the slams gained and the misfits suffered.

findings: 1D opening under five-card majors denies a five-card major; without caps the 2S tree's spade-raise branches fire on impossible five-spade opener layouts — majors capped at four.
Hero's pass does not end the auction (W could in principle balance); the single-else 2D tree is kept as an acceptable simplification given W's two prior passes.

deviations.2S: only three spades; a fresh suit at the two level implies four

N.note: fourth-seat 1D opening then a 2D rebid over 2C: sound minimum opener, roughly 11-14, five-plus diamonds, no five-card major

E.note: passed as dealer and stayed silent

W.note: passed in third seat and stayed silent

patched_doc.explanation: As a passed hand the 2C response was already a maximum effort, and partner's 2D rebid paints a minimum opener with long diamonds and, on this hand, a likely misfit: a singleton diamond opposite five or six of them. Passing takes the sure small minus or plus in 2D and avoids compounding a possible misfit. 2S with KQT is a three-card lie, but it is wonderfully economical: it shows where the values are, keeps 2NT and 3C in the frame, and often improves the contract a full level when partner has four spades. 3C insists on the self-sufficient-looking six-carder, which plays well opposite a small doubleton but commits to the three level opposite possible shortness. The hand is a lesson in escaping misfit auctions cheaply, and the double-dummy verdict will show which escape hatch pays.

findings: N's diamond band allowed a three-card suit (margin [3,3,0.2]), contradicting both the stated meaning ('diamonds at least as long as clubs') and the system: with three diamonds and four-plus clubs the opening is 1C — margin removed.

deviations.X: a competitive double on 8 HCP is light; the card wants genuine values in a live auction
deviations.2H: a direct two-level overcall promises 11+ with a good suit; this is 8 HCP and a shabby suit

N.note: 1D opening then 2C rebid: 11-16 (no reverse), diamonds at least as long as clubs, four-plus clubs

S.note: 1S response then 2D preference: 6-9 with four-plus spades and diamond tolerance, no game interest

E.note: passed over 1D and over 2C: no overcall, no takeout double

patched_doc.explanation: The opponents have limped into 2D on a minimum opener facing a weak preference, the classic moment to fight for the partscore, and the singleton diamond screams that partner has some values sitting quietly. But the ammunition is thin: 8 HCP, a mangy five-card heart suit, and both black-suit queens of doubtful worth. 2H puts the long suit on the table directly and makes West the declarer; the double is the flexible route, letting partner bid hearts from his side or even sit for penalties with fat diamonds behind the opening bidder. Both actions are lighter than the card allows and are flagged as such, so partner may compete a level higher expecting more. Passing is safe and sometimes right when the red-suit finesses are offside, but selling out to cozy two-level contracts is how imps leak away.

findings: Hero holds 9 HCP, not 8: the balancing double meets the card's 9-point balancing floor (12-3) with ideal 3-5-4-1 shape, so X is not a deviation — flag removed and explanation corrected.

X tree let opener run to 2S only with 14+ HCP, but North acts before East and runs on spade quality: the actual 11-count with KQJ76 ran to 2S in the open room — run branch moved first and keyed to spade honors.
2H tree's 2S-competition condition similarly loosened to a six-card suit or a maximum.

deviations.2H: meets the balancing point floor but A9762 falls well short of a good five-card suit

N.note: 1S opening then 2C rebid: 11-16 with five-plus spades and four-plus clubs (occasionally three)

S.note: 1NT response (6-10, at most two spades), then passed 2C showing club preference

E.note: passed over 1S and passed over 2C in direct seat: no overcall, no 1NT, no immediate double; up to a bad 14

patched_doc.explanation: The auction is dying in 2C and the balancing seat carries its traditional license: partner is marked with values when the opponents stop low in a semi-fit. With 9 HCP and 3-5-4-1 shape this double is a textbook balance — it meets the card's balancing floor exactly, with the singleton sitting in their suit — and it lets partner choose a red suit or convert for penalties with club length behind the bidder, though opener may simply run to 2S. The alternative 2H names the five-card suit directly and wins the declaration for this hand, but it stakes everything on a moth-eaten A9762, well short of the good suit even a balancing two-level bid wants, and it is flagged as such. Passing avoids the doubled minuses that punish balancing into a bad lie of the cards, but it sells out to a comfortable partscore whenever partner was sitting on the balance of the deck.

findings: Agree with the rejection: 7 HCP and 4=1=3=5 after Stayman finds no major makes 3NT routine under the card; the rooms' 3H/3NT divergence comes from Smolen-style methods that do not exist on this card, so there is no honest dilemma to pose.
